claims.text: Architecture preference for GW glitch classification is class-dependent: ViT outperforms CNN on some classes (e.g., Power_Line) while CNN retains advantages on others (e.g., Chirp).
ViT achieves significantly higher overall macro-F1 than CNN (0.723 vs 0.679, p=0.0002) on O3 data.
Rare-class comparison is statistically underpowered (aggregate power = 0.20), yielding insufficient evidence to determine whether ViT improves or degrades rare-class performance.
Architecture preference is not a monotonic function of training set size (Spearman rho = -0.034, p = 0.879 on O4; rho = -0.119, p = 0.59 on O3).
This is a controlled per-class comparison of ViT vs CNN under identical training conditions on Gravity Spy data; prior work reported only aggregate metrics.
First quantitative assessment of ViT vs CNN classification quality for continuous gravitational-wave search data quality using duty-cycle-based veto efficiency.
ViT's advantage on Power_Line is due to global self-attention capturing multi-frequency harmonic structure at 60 Hz and harmonics that local convolutional filters struggle to integrate.
CNN's advantage on rare classes is due to convolutional inductive bias (translation equivariance) providing better regularization from limited data than ViT's flexible attention.
Both models generalize to O4 data with <20% relative degradation in macro-F1, though per-class preferences do not replicate across observing runs.
At matched deadtime (22.4%), veto efficiency for CW searches is approximately equal (ViT 0.745 vs CNN 0.735).
Per-class evaluation, not aggregate accuracy, should be standard practice when deploying new architectures for imbalanced scientific classification tasks.
Temporal split (by GPS time with 60-second gaps) explains the accuracy gap between this work (~91-93%) and published Gravity Spy benchmarks (95-99%); random split recovers 95.4% accuracy.
Power_Line advantage is the most CW-relevant finding because 60 Hz harmonics directly contaminate CW searches in the most sensitive frequency bands.
Per-class architecture preferences are observing-run-specific observations, not universal laws.
